summary: DeVault argues that GitHub's design follows Microsoft's classic 'Embrace, Extend, Extinguish' strategy against open-source collaboration tools. GitHub embraced git but replaced its decentralized, email-based collaboration with proprietary pull requests, then Microsoft acquired it for $7.5 billion. He points to a Linux Foundation board member (who is also a Microsoft employee) calling email-based development a 'barrier to entry' as the 'extinguish' phase beginning. DeVault contrasts this with SourceHut's approach of improving the existing open ecosystem rather than replacing it.
key_points: GitHub co-opted git terminology like 'fork' and 'pull request,' replacing decentralized open workflows with proprietary, platform-locked equivalents
Microsoft's acquisition of GitHub and membership in the Linux Foundation are strategic moves to project influence over open-source development
A Microsoft employee advocating to move Linux kernel development away from email represents the 'extinguish' phase of EEE
SourceHut demonstrates that email-based development UX can be improved without abandoning the open, distributed protocol
The proper way to improve open ecosystems is to send improvements upstream, not build incompatible proprietary replacements
money_quotes: They took terminology which already had meaning — 'fork', meaning the creation a separate governing body and development upstream for a codebase — and replaced these decentralized, open systems with a completely incompatible system designed to keep you on GitHub.
Sarah Novotny, Microsoft employee, transitive owner of GitHub, and patroness saint of conflicts of interests.

Naturally, a representative of Microsoft, a company which has long waged war against open source, and GitHub, a company which explicitly built an incompatible proprietary system to extend git, would have an interest in dismantling the distributed, open system that git was designed for.
Instead of investing $7.5B into throwing the baby out with the bathwater, we've built interactive tutorials, designed better mailing lists, built web interfaces for patch submission.
This is how you improve the ecosystem, Microsoft. Take notes. Stick with the embrace, move your extending upstream, and forget about extinguish.
I represent sourcehut, a GitHub competitor which does what GitHub wouldn't — interoperate with open, distributed protocols, and in the form of 100% free and open-source software.
key_insight: GitHub's proprietary reimagining of git's decentralized collaboration tools was never about improving developer experience — it was about platform lock-in, and Microsoft is now leveraging that position to dismantle the open protocols git was built on.
